title: Matplotlib Intro
content:
Matplotlib Introduction

What is Matplotlib?

Matplotlib is a comprehensive library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations in Python. It is the most popular data visualization library in the Python ecosystem.

Why Use Matplotlib?

Matplotlib makes it easy to generate plots, histograms, power spectra, bar charts, errorcharts, scatterplots, etc., with just a few lines of code.

Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = np.sin(x)

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.title('Simple Sine Wave')
plt.xlabel('x axis')
plt.ylabel('y axis')
plt.show()

title: Basic Plotting
content:
Basic Plotting

Line Plots

The Pyplot interface provides a MATLAB-like interface. The plot() function is used to plot lines and markers.

Example

Line Plot Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
y = [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

plt.plot(x, y, color='green', marker='o', linestyle='dashed')
plt.show()

title: Customizing Plots
content:
Customizing Plots

Labels and Titles

You can add labels to the x and y axes using xlabel() and ylabel(), and a title using title().

Grid Lines

You can use grid() function to add grid lines to the plot.

Customization Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
y1 = [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]
y2 = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16]

plt.plot(x, y1, label='Square')
plt.plot(x, y2, label='Power of 2')

plt.xlabel('Number')
plt.ylabel('Value')
plt.title('Comparison of Functions')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

title: Subplots
content:
Multiple Plots (Subplots)

Creating Subplots

The subplots() function creates a figure and a set of subplots. It is a powerful way to organize multiple plots in one window.

Example

Subplots Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

ax1.plot(x, np.sin(x))
ax1.set_title('Sine Wave')

ax2.plot(x, np.cos(x), color='red')
ax2.set_title('Cosine Wave')

plt.show()

title: Scatter Plots
content:
Scatter Plots

Creating Scatter Plots

Scatter plots are used to observe relationship between variables and uses dots to represent the relationship.

Example

Scatter Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.random.rand(50)
y = np.random.rand(50)
colors = np.random.rand(50)
sizes = 1000 * np.random.rand(50)

plt.scatter(x, y, c=colors, s=sizes, alpha=0.5)
plt.colorbar()  # Show color scale
plt.show()

title: Bar Charts
content:
Bar Charts

Creating Bar Charts

The bar() function takes arguments that describes the layout of the bars.

Example

Bar Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

categories = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D']
values = [10, 24, 15, 30]

plt.bar(categories, values, color=['blue', 'red', 'green', 'orange'])
plt.title('Category Comparison')
plt.show()

title: Histograms
content:
Histograms

Creating Histograms

A histogram is a graph showing frequency distributions. It shows the number of observations within each given interval.

Example

Histogram Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

data = np.random.randn(1000)

plt.hist(data, bins=30, edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Frequency Distribution')
plt.show()

title: Pie Charts
content:
Pie Charts

Creating Pie Charts

The pie() function is used to create pie charts from data.

Example

Pie Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

labels = ['Python', 'Java', 'C++', 'Ruby']
sizes = [45, 30, 15, 10]
explode = (0.1, 0, 0, 0)  # offset the first slice

plt.pie(sizes, explode=explode, labels=labels, autopct='%1.1f%%', shadow=True)
plt.title('Language Popularity')
plt.show()

title: Advanced Customization
content:
Advanced Customization

Styles and Colors

Matplotlib comes with several pre-defined styles or you can define your own.

Example

Style Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

plt.style.use('ggplot')

x = np.linspace(0, 5, 10)
y = x ** 2

plt.plot(x, y, 'r--')
plt.annotate('Local Min', xy=(1, 1), xytext=(3, 10), arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', shrink=0.05))
plt.show()

title: Box Plots
content:
Box Plots

Creating Box Plots

Box plots graphically depict groups of numerical data through their quartiles, highlighting the median and outliers.

Example

Box Plot Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

data1 = np.random.normal(100, 10, 200)
data2 = np.random.normal(90, 20, 200)
data3 = np.random.normal(80, 30, 200)

box_data = [data1, data2, data3]

plt.boxplot(box_data, labels=['Group A', 'Group B', 'Group C'])
plt.title('Box Plot Comparison')
plt.ylabel('Values')
plt.show()

title: Error Bars
content:
Error Bars

Plotting with Error Bars

Error bars are useful to problem solvers because they show the confidence or precision in a set of measurements or calculated values.

Example

Errorbar Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 50)
dy = 0.8
y = np.sin(x) + dy * np.random.randn(50)

plt.errorbar(x, y, yerr=dy, fmt='.k')
plt.title('Error Bar Plot')
plt.show()

title: Contour Plots
content:
Contour Plots

Visualizing 3D Data in 2D

Contour plots (sometimes called Level Plots) are a way to show a three-dimensional surface on a two-dimensional plane.

Example

Contour Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def f(x, y):
    return np.sin(np.sqrt(x ** 2 + y ** 2))

x = np.linspace(-6, 6, 30)
y = np.linspace(-6, 6, 30)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = f(X, Y)

plt.contourf(X, Y, Z, 20, cmap='RdGy')
plt.colorbar()
plt.title('Filled Contour Plot')
plt.show()

title: 3D Plotting
content:
3D Plotting

Creating 3D Plots

Matplotlib includes a 3D toolkit `mplot3d` which allows you to create three-dimensional plots.

Example

3D Surface Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 50)
y = np.linspace(-5, 5, 50)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = np.sin(np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2))

ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, cmap='viridis')
ax.set_title('3D Surface Plot')
plt.show()

title: Text & Annotations
content:
Text and Annotations

Adding Text and Arrows

You can add arbitrary text or annotate arbitrary points on your axes by using text() and annotate() methods.

Example

Annotation Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
ax.plot(x, np.sin(x))

ax.annotate('Local Maximum',
            xy=(np.pi/2, 1),
            xytext=(10, 4),
            textcoords='offset points',
            arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', shrink=0.05))

ax.text(8, -0.5, 'Example Text', style='italic', bbox={'facecolor': 'red', 'alpha': 0.5, 'pad': 10})
plt.show()

title: Twin Axes
content:
Twin Axes (Secondary Axis)

Plotting with Different Scales

Sometimes it's necessary to plot two different datasets with different scales on the same graph, using a secondary axis.

Example

Twin Axes Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y1 = np.exp(x)
y2 = np.sin(x)

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()

color = 'tab:red'
ax1.set_xlabel('time (s)')
ax1.set_ylabel('exp', color=color)
ax1.plot(x, y1, color=color)
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color)

ax2 = ax1.twinx()  # instantiate a second axes that shares the same x-axis

color = 'tab:blue'
ax2.set_ylabel('sin', color=color)
ax2.plot(x, y2, color=color)
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Heatmaps
content:
Heatmaps

Visualizing Matrix Data

A heatmap displays a 2D matrix of data, representing the values by colors using the imshow() function.

Example

Heatmap Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

data = np.random.rand(10, 10)

plt.imshow(data, cmap='hot', interpolation='nearest')
plt.colorbar()
plt.title('Heatmap Example')
plt.show()

title: Saving Figures
content:
Saving Figures

Exporting Your Work

Once you have created a plot, you'll likely want to save it as an image file (e.g., PNG, JPG, PDF) using savefig().

Example

Savefig Example
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x))
plt.title('My Saved Plot')

# Save the figure
plt.savefig('my_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')

# You can also use other extensions
# plt.savefig('my_plot.pdf')

print("Figure saved successfully!")

title: Course Summary
content:
Matplotlib Course Summary 🎉

You Did It! 🎉

You have successfully completed the expanded Matplotlib course! You are now equipped with comprehensive data visualization skills.

  • Basics: Line Plots, Subplots, Customizations.
  • Charts & Plots: Scatter, Bar, Pie, Histograms, Box Plots.
  • Advanced Elements: 3D Plots, Contour Plots, Heatmaps, Dual Axes.
  • Polishing: Error Bars, Annotations, and Saving Figures.

Your journey into data visualization is now complete! Remember to explore Seaborn or Plotly for more advanced graphics!
